The Ferrofluid

A new series begins with a liquid that should not exist: ink-black, glossy, and magnetic. Your cursor is the magnet. Hold it near the pool and the fluid climbs; the surface you see is rebuilt from the particles, from scratch, every frame.

Scroll down to read ↓

Ferrofluid is a real substance with a science-fiction résumé: an oil crowded with magnetite grains so small (about ten nanometers) that thermal jostling keeps them suspended forever, so the liquid as a whole responds to a magnet while staying honestly liquid. Hold a magnet underneath a dish of it and the surface breaks into a regular crown of spikes — the Rosensweig instability, the signature image of the stuff.

This article builds one in a browser tab. The fluid machinery is borrowed whole from the water and lava lamp articles — particles, grid sort, double-density relaxation. What's new is the antagonist (a magnet, stated as a force) and the rendering: instead of thresholding a blurry field into pixels, the surface is extracted as geometry — line segments, meshed on the GPU every frame by the two-dimensional cousin of one of the most-used algorithms in graphics.

Aside · Rocket fuel that follows orders

Ferrofluid was invented at NASA. In 1963, Steve Papell was working on a problem of weightlessness — rocket propellant floats away from the pump inlet in zero g — and proposed grinding magnetic particles into the fuel so a magnetic field could herd it where the plumbing needed it. The propellant idea lost out to other solutions; the liquid escaped the space program and built an industry of loudspeaker coolants, rotary seals, and mesmerizing desk toys. Ronald Rosensweig, who developed the theory (and the word ferrohydrodynamics), explained the spikes the fluid grows in a field — and they now carry his name.

Step 1 · Three forcesWhy spikes, of all shapes

The spike crown is a three-way fight, and the simulation needs exactly the three fighters:

Gravity wants the surface flat. Surface tension — the Akinci-style cohesion force the lava lamp added to keep its wax in one piece — wants the surface small: it charges for every extra centimeter of interface, so it too votes flat, and rounds off anything that tries to be pointy. The magnet disagrees. A magnetizable fluid is pulled toward where the field is strongest — the force is the gradient of the field's energy density, F ∝ ∇|B|² — and for a dipole that gradient has a useful anatomy: 

|B|² ∝ (3cos²θ + 1) / r⁶     // strongest on the dipole's axis
F = ∇|B|²                     // toward the magnet — and sideways, toward its axis

The radial part pulls fluid toward the magnet. The angular part is the spike-maker: off-axis fluid is pushed toward the axis, so instead of a broad mound the fluid gathers into a column aligned with the field. Tension fights the column's sharp tip, gravity fights its height, and the standoff is a spike of definite size. (In a real dish the fight repeats across the whole surface and the spikes space themselves out — their spacing is set by the same gravity-versus-tension balance, the capillary length. With one moving magnet, this demo grows them one or two at a time.)

In the force kernel the magnet is six lines — the same "constraints are forces" move as the dome, the tank walls, and the lamp's buoyancy. Nothing else in the SPH stack learned anything about magnetism.

Step 2 · Geometry from a fieldMarching squares

The lava lamp rendered its wax by splatting particles into a blurry density field and shading every pixel past a threshold. That worked because wax is soft and the lamp is dim. A ferrofluid spike is the opposite client: glossy, ink-dark, with a silhouette so crisp it's the whole show. For that, we want the surface itself — as geometry, not as a smoothstep.

The field already knows where the surface is: it's the contour where the splat sum crosses the threshold. Marching squares extracts it. Walk every 2×2 cell of field texels and ask the four corners: above the threshold or below? Sixteen possible answers, and each one dictates how the contour crosses the cell — through this edge and that one, with the crossing point interpolated from the corner values. Each cell volunteers its zero, one, or two little line segments; together they chain into the complete surface.

That is the extracted mesh, alone — every cyan stroke is one segment from one cell, and the chain re-derives itself every frame as the fluid moves. On the GPU the extraction is one compute pass: each cell that finds a crossing appends its segments to a buffer with an atomic counter — the same wait-free append as the gravity series' scatters — and a one-thread pass converts the final count into the arguments of an indirect draw, so the segment total is decided, allocated, and drawn without the CPU ever hearing the number.

Aside · The most litigated triangles in graphics

The 3D version — marching cubes, 256 corner cases instead of 16 — was published by Lorensen and Cline in 1987 and became the default way to pull surfaces out of CT scans, MRI volumes, and every implicit field since; by some counts it's among the most-cited papers in the field. It was also patented, which so annoyed the research community that a workaround ("marching tetrahedra") was invented largely to dodge the license. The patent expired in 2005; the algorithm is now everyone's.

Deep dive: the saddle, or sixteen cases minus two honest ones

Fourteen of the sixteen corner patterns are unambiguous. The two "saddle" cases — diagonal corners inside, the other diagonal outside — admit two readings: one blob pinching off, or two blobs barely touching. The cell's four corners genuinely cannot tell the difference; the demo breaks the tie by sampling the field once more at the cell's center. Watch a blob split in the mesh view and you are watching saddle cases change their minds frame by frame — for a pinching ferrofluid neck, that tie-break is the moment of separation.

Step 3 · AssemblyThe dish

Everything together: the SPH pool, the cursor-magnet, and the per-frame mesh — filled and shaded this time. The shading cheats honestly: the field's gradient stands in for a surface normal (dense interior = tall), which buys the glossy highlight that makes the liquid read as ferrofluid rather than tar. Drag the tension to zero and the spikes dissolve into spray; drag it high and the fluid balls up and resists the magnet; find the middle and you get the standoff the real liquid lives at. The view button exposes each stage — final, mesh, the raw field with its threshold contour, and the particles underneath it all.

Worth noticing while you play: the mesh view's segment count (in the readout) holds roughly steady while the fluid is calm, then spikes when the surface grows spikes — geometry is being created exactly where the surface buys complexity. And nothing remembers the mesh between frames. Like the gravity series' trees, rebuilt from scratch every step because rebuilding parallelizes and updating doesn't, the surface is cheaper to re-derive than to maintain.

CodaWhere this series goes

This article is the 2D proof of a pipeline: particles → field → geometry, every frame, all on the GPU. Each arrow has an obvious next rung. The fluid wants a second phase (the lava lamp's water, actually simulated, so buoyancy emerges instead of being decreed). The meshing wants a third dimension — marching squares becomes marching cubes, segments become triangles, and the prefix-sum machinery from the gravity series already knows how to allocate them. And sharp features want a sharper extractor: marching cubes rounds off exactly the spike tips this fluid is proudest of, which is the problem dual contouring exists to solve. Particles first, then surfaces, then — as always in these series — light.

Matter in a Browser Tab, part one · the gravity, light, and music series that precede it

Standing on the shoulders of: Papell, "Low viscosity magnetic fluid" (1963/65) · Cowley & Rosensweig, "The interfacial stability of a ferromagnetic fluid" (1967) · Lorensen & Cline, "Marching Cubes" (1987) · Akinci, Akinci & Teschner, "Versatile Surface Tension and Adhesion for SPH Fluids" (2013) · Clavet, Beaudoin & Poulin, "Particle-based Viscoelastic Fluid Simulation" (2005)
